title: __init__ and Attributes
subtitle: Automatically set up every object the right way
learning_objectives: Define __init__ to automatically set attributes when an object is created
Create well-structured classes that require initial values

sections.heading: The __init__ Method
sections.content: Setting attributes manually after creating an object is error-prone. The __init__ method solves this — it runs automatically every time you create a new object. You can require initial values and set them all in one place.
sections.code_example:
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name, breed, age):
        self.name = name
        self.breed = breed
        self.age = age

# Now creating a Dog REQUIRES the three values
rex = Dog("Rex", "Labrador", 3)
print(rex.name)   # Rex
print(rex.breed)  # Labrador
print(rex.age)    # 3

sections.heading: What is 'self'?
sections.content: self is a reference to the specific object being created or used. When you write self.name = name, you are saying: store this value on THIS particular object. Python passes self automatically — you never need to pass it yourself when calling a method. The name self is just a convention; it could technically be any name, but always use self.
sections.code_example:
class Person:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        self.name = name   # stored ON this object
        self.age = age

alice = Person("Alice", 30)
bob = Person("Bob", 25)

# Each object remembers its own values
print(alice.name, alice.age)  # Alice 30
print(bob.name, bob.age)      # Bob 25

sections.heading: More Attribute Patterns
sections.content: You can set default values, compute attributes, or do other setup in __init__.
sections.code_example:
class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, owner, initial_balance=0):
        self.owner = owner
        self.balance = initial_balance
        self.transactions = []  # starts as empty list

account = BankAccount("Alice", 1000)
print(account.owner)
print(account.balance)
print(account.transactions)

exercises.title: Add __init__ to a Car
exercises.prompt: Define a Car class with __init__(self, make, model, year). Create a car object: my_car = Car('Toyota', 'Corolla', 2022). Print: '2022 Toyota Corolla'.
exercises.solution:
class Car:
    def __init__(self, make, model, year):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

my_car = Car("Toyota", "Corolla", 2022)
print(f"{my_car.year} {my_car.make} {my_car.model}")
hints: Also: self.model = model and self.year = year
Then my_car.year, my_car.make, my_car.model will all work

exercises.title: Class with Default Attribute
exercises.prompt: Define a Player class with __init__(self, name) that sets self.name = name and self.score = 0. Create player1 = Player('Alice'). Print player1.name and player1.score.
exercises.starter_code:
class Player:
    def __init__(self, name):
        # Set name from parameter, score starts at 0
        pass

player1 = Player("Alice")
print(player1.name)
print(player1.score)
exercises.solution:
class Player:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        self.score = 0

player1 = Player("Alice")
print(player1.name)
print(player1.score)
hints: No need to pass score when creating the object

quiz.question: When does __init__ run?
options: Automatically every time you create a new object
Only when you call it manually
quiz.explanation: __init__ is called automatically by Python every time you create a new object using ClassName(). You never call __init__ yourself — Python calls it for you.

quiz.question: What does 'self.name = name' inside __init__ do?
options: Creates a global variable called name
Stores the value of 'name' as an attribute on this specific object
Defines a new function called name
quiz.explanation: self.name = name stores the value of the parameter 'name' as an attribute on the specific object being created. After this, you can access it later as object.name.

completion_message: Well done! __init__ is one of the most important concepts in OOP — you're mastering it!
